one_line_takeaway: EC extends contrastive unlearning to a multi-layer, deeply-supervised architecture, substantially reducing representation-level similarity to the original model (e.g., ImageNet-1K CKA 38.68 vs CU's 69.52) while keeping retain-set utility in a comparable range to other strong baselines, and works as a plug-in that improves CKA/IDI for DUCK and COLA.
key_finding: On ImageNet-1K Random-100 (ResNet-50, Table 1), EC attains FA=0.00, RA=72.63, TFA=0.00, TRA=73.84, CKA=38.68, |IDI|=0.051, H-Mean=85.75 -- the lowest CKA and |IDI| and the highest H-Mean among methods that preserve reasonable retain utility (excluding SalUn and RL, which achieve low CKA -- 9.10 and 3.39 respectively -- but collapse retain accuracy to 39.84 and 9.56). The next-best representation-forgetting baseline, CU, reaches CKA=69.52, |IDI|=0.403, H-Mean=70.68 at RA=75.83. On CIFAR-100 (Table 2), EC gives FA=0.00, RA=95.63, TFA=0.00, TRA=74.80, CKA=61.98, |IDI|=0.291, H-Mean=71.23, versus CU's CKA=66.93, |IDI|=0.539, H-Mean=62.94. Layer-wise CKA (Figure 2) shows EC diverges most at deeper layers (Layer 4.2 CKA=38.68 vs CU's 69.52), while baselines like PL, DUCK, SCAR, DELETE, COLA stay above CKA~89-99 across all three measured sub-layers, indicating their forgetting is concentrated at the output layer. As a plug-in (Table 3, ImageNet-1K Random-100): DUCK alone has CKA=90.15/|IDI|=0.538; DUCK(+EC) improves to CKA=87.67/|IDI|=0.511 and also improves RA (71.21->72.02) and TRA (72.34->73.00); the appendix reports DUCK(+EC) raising H-Mean from 44.65 to 50.12. COLA(+EC) shows a smaller CKA/|IDI| improvement (89.28->89.15, 0.867->0.846) with utility roughly unchanged. Ablations (Table 4) show removing the layer-wise CE loss raises CKA to 59.28 and |IDI| to 0.205 (from 38.68/0.051), and removing the EC modules entirely destabilizes optimization, producing a raw (signed) IDI of -0.233, interpreted by the authors as over-forgetting.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Erase at the Core (EC), a machine-unlearning framework for image classifiers that attaches auxiliary 'EC modules' to intermediate layers of a backbone (e.g., ResNet-50, Swin-Tiny) and applies, at each supervision point, (1) a multi-layer contrastive unlearning loss that pulls forget-set embeddings toward the retain-set embedding manifold, and (2) a cross-entropy loss on the retain set to preserve utility, with progressively larger weights assigned to deeper layers. This extends the single-layer contrastive unlearning objective of Contrastive Unlearning (CU, Zhang et al. 2025) to a multi-layer / deep-supervision regime (inspired by Contrastive Deep Supervision, Zhang et al. 2022), and EC is shown to be usable as a model-agnostic plug-in on top of other representation-based unlearning baselines (DUCK, COLA).
threat_model: None. This is not an attack/defense paper and has no adversary model. The motivating problem is a compliance/utility one: GDPR 'right to be forgotten' requires that a trained model's dependence on a designated forget set be removed while retaining performance on the rest of the data. The paper's own diagnostic concept, 'superficial forgetting,' describes a measurement gap (logit-level forgetting without representation-level forgetting) rather than an adversarial capability; the paper does not construct or evaluate an attacker exploiting this gap (it cites, but does not itself run, linear-probing attacks from other work, e.g. Jung et al. 2025 / Jeon et al. 2026, as related evidence of residual information).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: the evaluation provides empirical evidence of representation-level forgetting but no formal erasure guarantee (no theoretical unlearning bound); multi-layer supervision adds computational overhead (extra EC modules, SupCon pretraining phase, and per-layer forward/backward passes) relative to single-point unlearning methods. Additional limitations evident from the text: EC's utility trade-off is baseline-dependent -- e.g., EC has lower RA/TRA than CU on ImageNet-1K (72.63 vs 75.83) despite better representation-level forgetting, so the paper's own H-Mean aggregate is doing real work to declare EC 'best' since no single raw metric dominates across all baselines; EC modules are attached only after Stages 1-3 (not Stage 4) by design choice, and the CKA/IDI reference values used for comparison (e.g., the Retrained model's own CKA of 86.19 relative to Original on ImageNet-1K) are nonzero, meaning even the gold-standard retrained-from-scratch model doesn't reach CKA=0, complicating interpretation of 'how low is low enough.' All experiments use a single seed/run configuration as far as reported (no variance/confidence intervals given in the excerpted tables).

quantitative_results: ImageNet-1K Random-100, ResNet-50: EC FA=0.00, RA=72.63, TFA=0.00, TRA=73.84, CKA=38.68, |IDI|=0.051, H-Mean=85.75
ImageNet-1K Random-100, ResNet-50: CU FA=0.00, RA=75.83, TFA=0.00, TRA=75.49, CKA=69.52, |IDI|=0.403, H-Mean=70.68
Layer-4.2 (penultimate) CKA on ImageNet-1K: EC=38.68 vs CU=69.52 vs DUCK=90.15 vs COLA=89.28 vs DELETE=97.19
Plug-in results (ImageNet-1K): DUCK alone CKA=90.15/|IDI|=0.538/RA=71.21/TRA=72.34 -> DUCK(+EC) CKA=87.67/|IDI|=0.511/RA=72.02/TRA=73.00, H-Mean 44.65->50.12
Plug-in results (ImageNet-1K): COLA alone CKA=89.28/|IDI|=0.867 -> COLA(+EC) CKA=89.15/|IDI|=0.846
Ablation (ImageNet-1K): full EC CKA=38.68/|IDI|=0.051; w/o layer-wise CE CKA=59.28/|IDI|=0.205; w/o EC modules CKA=63.07/raw IDI=-0.233 (over-forgetting); +final backbone blocks CKA=56.03/|IDI|=0.100
ImageNet-1K Top-100 (appendix): EC reports highest H-Mean=69.98 with lowest CKA=77.89 among utility-preserving methods
Swin-Tiny ImageNet-1K Random-100 (appendix): EC (CE-at-final-only variant) reports highest H-Mean=62.51 and lowest |IDI|=0.477 among utility-preserving methods
